findings.description: touch() fetches the session without purging or checking expiry and unconditionally resets expires_at, so an already-expired (but not-yet-purged) session is resurrected and returns True — directly contradicting get(), which purges first and would return None for that same token.

user_sessions() does not call _purge_expired() (unlike get() and active_count()), so it returns expired sessions whenever a purge has not run recently, making the result non-deterministic and inconsistent with the rest of the store's expiry semantics.

user_sessions() returns live references to the internal mutable Session dataclasses, letting a caller mutate expires_at/user_id and corrupt store state (encapsulation leak; borders on design rather than a pure logic defect).

create() writes self._sessions[token] without checking for an existing key, so a token collision (astronomically unlikely with 62^32 space) would silently clobber another user's active session instead of regenerating.
Out-of-scope note (defer to critic-security): tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic random module rather than secrets, making session tokens predictable/guessable; not a logic defect.
